text: Iron deficiency in athletes is caused by three main mechanisms: increased iron demand, elevated iron loss, and blockage of iron absorption due to hepcidin bursts after exercise.
In healthy male and female athletes over 15 years, serum ferritin values below 15 mcg/l indicate empty iron stores, and values from 15 to 30 mcg/l indicate low iron stores.
A serum ferritin cut-off of 30 mcg/l is the appropriate threshold for identifying iron deficiency in athletes over 15 years old.
Elite athletes should attain a serum ferritin value of at least 50 mcg/l prior to altitude training, as iron demands are increased at altitude.
Baseline blood tests for monitoring iron status in athletes should include haemoglobin, haematocrit, mean cellular volume, mean cellular haemoglobin, and serum ferritin, repeated twice per year.
Athletes with repeatedly low ferritin values benefit from intermittent oral iron supplementation rather than continuous daily supplementation.
Long-term daily oral iron intake or intravenous iron supplementation in athletes with normal or high ferritin values is not beneficial and may be harmful.
All types of iron deficiency, not just iron deficiency anemia, can impair physical performance in athletes and should be treated.
